I. LAW : MEANING AND DEFINITIONS

The word "Law  has been derived from the Teutonic word  Lag , which means  definite . On

tris basis, Law can be simply defined as a definite rule of conduct and human relations. It also

neans a uniform rule of conduct which is applicable equally to all the people of the State. Law
pescribes general conditions of human activity in the state.

Jurists and political scientists have offered several definitions of Law :

 Law is the command of the sovereign.   It is the command of the superior to an inferior  and force is the

The State converted several moral and religious rules into its laws. Hence, Religion and Morality
have also been important sources of Law.

3. Legislation/Statutes or Statutory Law : Since the emergence of legislatures (the first
parliament came to be established in England in 1295), legislation has emerged as the chief
source of Law. Traditionally, the State depended upon customs and the decrees of the King for
regulating the behaviour of the people. Later on, the legislature emerged as an organ of the
govenment engaged in transforming the customary rules into definite and enacted rules of
behaviour in society. The King, as the sovereign, started giving these his approval. Gradually
legislation became the chief source of law-making and

later on the legislature got recognition as
the Legal Sovereign i.e. law-making organ of the State.

of law. Legislation has come to be recognised as the chief means for the formulation of the will of

5. Judicial Decisions : In contemporary times, Judicial Decisions have also :
important source of Law. It is the responsibility of the courts to interpret and apply , h:

specific cases. The courts settle the disputes of the people in the cases that come before them p, !

decisions of the courts (the judicial decisions) are binding on the parties to the dispute. Ty
also get accepted as laws for future cases. But not all judicial decisions are laws. Only the udiy
decisions given by the apex court or the courts which stand recognised as the Courts of Reg;

like the Supreme Court and High Courts of India, are recognised and used as laws F !!l   

Courts can settle the future cases on the basis of such judicial decisions.

Further, while interpreting and applying laws, the courts delimit the nature and Scope of &
provisions of Law. This also involves law-making.

Thus Judicial Decisions now-a-days stand accepted as a good source of Law.

1. Constitutional Law: Constitutional Law is the supreme law of the

2. Municipal Law. Municipal Law means the body of domestic or internal law of , son
state which is in operation in each state. In the context of International law, Mu idpal i
means, the system of law made, or followed, and implemented by each state Within i Ly
territory. Municipal Law includes not only law at the national level but also the law 4 the Oy
provinial, territorial regional or local levels in each state of the world. In the context of nag
law, municipal law refers to the systems of local laws at work within the national boungyy.
3. National Law. It means the system of law of the state which is being enforced by t, )
through its law courts.

municipal law refers to the systems of local laws at work within the national boungyy.
3. National Law. It means the system of law of the state which is being enforced by t, )
through its law courts. It applies to all citizens and the whole territory of the state. Tt js thelg,
established in a single country and applies to people of the state. In contemporary times g,
laws passed by the legislature of the state, implemented by the executive and adjudicateq .
by the judiciary, together constitute the system of national law.

4. Statutes or Statute Law or Statutory Law : The laws enacted by the legislature of ey
state are called statutes or statutory laws. A statute is a written formal enactment of a legislatiye
authority or law-making body that is binding upon and governs the people, grouy
organizations, associations and institutions of the people and their government. Statutory lays
are also binding on the law-makers and they also obey these. These are passed by the legislature
in accordance with the powers given to it by the constitution of the state. The Constitution aly
prescribes the procedure of passing laws. The Constitution of India lays down that the Unim
Parliament can pass laws on the subjects listed in the Union List (ii) and the Concurrent List
addition, it can also make laws on any such subject which is not listed in any of the Lists. Italw
lays down the procedure of law-making. The Union Parliament passes bills which becomelas
when signed by the President of India.

5. Ordinary Law: It includes the national law or the municipal law. It is made by &
government and it determines and regulates the conduct and behaviour of the people. It 125
down the relations among the people and their associations, groups and institutions. 'Dt
legislature makes laws, the executive implements these and judiciary interprets and appllfs
these in various cases. Ordinary Law stands classified into two parts: Private Law and Pubi

6. Ordinances. An Ordinance can be defined as temporary or ad-hoc rules of law which is
needed at a time when the parliament is not in session.

h it is at the same
ime one of the parties interested. 
6. Ordinances. An Ordinance can be defined as temporary or ad-hoc rules of law which is
needed at a time when the parliament is not in session. An ordinance is issued by the head of
state for satisfying an urgent need for law. The ordinance thus issued, immediately gets
implemented as law. However, it can remain in operation for a defined and specified period
during which it is either converted into a regular law or a new law, which takes into account the
provision of the ordinance, is enacted by the legislature. In case the Parliament decides not to
convert it into a law, the ordinance lapses. Parliament can adopt or reject any ordinance. The
head of the State can also issue a new ordinance for terminating an existing ordinance. Hence
ordinance can be defined as a temporary law. The executive head of state can issue ordinances
but this does not mean that he can rule by ordinances. Ordinance-making power is an
emergency/ad-hoc arrangement; it cannot take the place of law-making by the Parliament.

animp

7. International Law. International Law is that body of law which relates to the behaviour,
actions and interactions of the states at the international level. Since all the states are sovereign
states and no law can be imposed on them, International Law is a system of law among the
states and not above the states. It does not enjoy any legal sanction and it is not backed by force.
Punishment cannot be given to any state for any violation of rules of International Law because
each state is a sovereign state . The states obey the rules of International law because these are
deemedvessen al and useful for the conduct of relations among nations. Most of the rules of
1m n ahom\ Law are either cu'stomary rules or treaty-based rules. International Law relates to
such issues or subjects as Treaties among States, War, Peace, Diplomacy, State Succession, State
